title: Module 6 Quiz
description: Test your understanding of adversity, coping, injury, mental toughness, and habit formation.

blocks.question: Match each grief stage to the athlete example:

pairs.right: "I'll rest two days and be fine. I've trained through worse."
"Why me? I did everything right. This isn't fair."
"If I just cross-train, I won't lose any fitness."
"What's the point? Everyone else will be ahead of me."
"This is where I am. What can I do from here?"

blocks.explanation: Right. Naming the stage creates distance between you and the emotion — from BEING angry to OBSERVING anger.

blocks.prompt: Sort each coping scenario into Problem-Focused or Emotion-Focused:

items.text: Journaling about frustration after a DNF
Using the Teammate Test after a bad workout
Adjusting nutrition plan to fix GI issues
Practicing 6-2-7 breathing after getting race results you can't change

feedback.correct: Exactly. Problem-focused when you can take concrete action. Emotion-focused when the situation is outside your control and you manage your relationship with it.
feedback.incorrect: Problem-focused = taking action to change the situation (PT sessions, alternative races, nutrition adjustments). Emotion-focused = managing feelings about what you can't change (journaling, Teammate Test, breathing after fixed results).

blocks.question: Select ALL four types of support an injured athlete needs (Cheadle):
blocks.explanation: All four: Psychological, Physical, Informational, Motivational. Most injured athletes get one or two — the missing ones are usually psychological and motivational.

options.text: Psychological support — validation of the emotional experience
Physical support — proper medical care and rehabilitation
Informational support — clear answers, realistic timelines
Motivational support — reminders you will come back

blocks.question: Match each of the 4 C's to the module that builds it:

pairs.right: Module 1 — Gap Check, 6-2-7, Know Your Zone

blocks.explanation: Each C maps to a module. Find the weakest. Go build it. One tool, five days.

blocks.question: Complete the Daily 5 sequence: Minute 1 = ___, Minute 2 = ___, Minute 3 = ___, Minute 4 = ___, Minute 5 = ___.
blocks.explanation: Breathe. See. Speak. Affirm. Reflect. Five minutes. Every day.

blocks.question: Stage 2 of the competence model (Conscious Incompetence) feels like:
blocks.explanation: Right. Awareness has outpaced skill. You can see the errors now, which feels terrible — but seeing them is the prerequisite for fixing them. This is where most people quit. Don't.

options.text: Getting worse — you can see errors you couldn't see before

blocks.prompt: An athlete is 4 weeks post-surgery on a meniscus repair. Physical therapy is going well. When asked how they're doing, they say: "I'm fine. I just need to push through it. I'll be racing again in two weeks." Their PT says the timeline is 8-10 more weeks.

options.label: A
options.result: Half right. The stage identification is correct — denial. But problem-focused coping aimed at accelerating an uncontrollable timeline is exactly how athletes re-injure themselves. The healing timeline isn't controllable.

options.label: B
options.result: Correct. The stage is denial — "I'm fine" and an unrealistic timeline are textbook markers. The healing process is not controllable, so emotion-focused coping is appropriate: name the stage, accept the real timeline, use the Teammate Test, and ensure they have all four support types (psychological, physical, informational, motivational).

options.label: C
options.result: Dangerous. Confusing denial with toughness is how athletes re-injure themselves. Mental toughness includes knowing when to stop, following medical guidance, and working WITH reality — not overriding it with willpower. Pushing a 10-week rehab into 2 weeks isn't toughness. It's recklessness.

blocks.question: 7. Which of the following correctly lists the 4 C's of mental toughness?
blocks.explanation: Correct. Clough's 4 C's: Control (life and emotions), Commitment (goal-setting and persistence), Challenge (seeing threat as opportunity), and Confidence (self-belief and interpersonal). These aren't personality traits — they're trainable skills.
